officeCatalog.name: Sovereign Crown Office
officeCatalog.summary: The ruling crown office of the Maldives during monarchical periods (rendered as Sultan or Queen by holder profile).

officeCatalog.name: President
officeCatalog.summary: Head of state and government in the republican era.

officeCatalog.name: Furadaana (Furadhaana)
officeCatalog.summary: Royal ministerial council/cabinet around the sovereign, with office composition changing over time.

officeCatalog.name: Kilege
officeCatalog.summary: Senior office in the Furadaana, often described as head of ministers/chief ministerial figure.

officeCatalog.name: Bandeyri
officeCatalog.summary: Court/palace portfolio tied to royal household and palace personnel, with broad state functions in later periods.

officeCatalog.name: Velaanaa
officeCatalog.summary: Revenue-linked office associated with fines, duties, and other fiscal collections in early records.

officeCatalog.name: Faiylia
officeCatalog.summary: Office tied to royal stores and custody/receipt of royal goods.

officeCatalog.name: Dhoshimeynaa
officeCatalog.summary: Military-linked office; described in different periods as army-general/chief defense portfolio.

officeCatalog.name: Hakuraa
officeCatalog.summary: Ministerial office associated with court audience/protocol and, in later descriptions, public works.

officeCatalog.name: Fandiyaaru
officeCatalog.summary: Chief judge/Qadi office in the pre-modern state and monarchy-era judicial structure.

officeCatalog.name: Handeygiri
officeCatalog.summary: High office in later monarchical administration; role descriptions vary from religious portfolio to treasury-central authority.

officeCatalog.name: Faamuladheyri
officeCatalog.summary: Treasury-linked office in descriptions of traditional government portfolios.

officeCatalog.name: Maafaiy
officeCatalog.summary: Revenue office identified as receiver-general in historical descriptions.

officeCatalog.name: Faashanaa
officeCatalog.summary: Police/security portfolio in late monarchical office descriptions.

officeCatalog.name: Naib (Naibu / Al-Naib)
officeCatalog.summary: Deputy/viceroy-style court office title; appears in elite genealogical lines and 19th-century cabinet descriptions.

officeTimeline.label: 14th century (Ibn Battuta era)
officeTimeline.summary: Court records point to a structured cabinet around the vizier and a distinct judicial office (Qadi/Fandiyaaru).

officeTimeline.label: Early 17th century (Pyrard account)
officeTimeline.summary: Furadaana listed with Kilege, Velaanaa, Hakuraa, Fandiyaaru, Dhoshimeynaa, Faiylia, and Bandeyri among principal offices.

officeTimeline.label: 1834 (Christopher account)
officeTimeline.summary: Cabinet descriptions include Kilege, Faiylia, Hakuraa, Bandeyri, Naibu, and Dhoshimeyna as senior posts, with Fandiyaaru still distinct.

officeTimeline.label: 1879 (Bell account)
officeTimeline.summary: A compressed model is reported: three key ministers plus the chief judge; Handeygiri and Faashanaa appear among powerful offices.

officeTimeline.label: 20th century transition
officeTimeline.summary: Constitutional and legal centralization reduced older court-office autonomy; monarchy ended in 1968 and republican executive offices became dominant.

officeTimeline.label: Republican era
officeTimeline.summary: Traditional office titles persist in social memory and elite naming, while formal state leadership is under the presidency and modern institutions.

storyTrails.title: Dhiyamigili -> Huraagey Transition
storyTrails.summary: Walk through late-Dhiyamigili succession dynamics and the long Huraagey consolidation arc into the 20th century.

steps.title: Late Dhiyamigili consolidation
steps.note: Court legitimacy and succession continuity remain centered in the Dhiyamigili line.

steps.title: Ibrahim Iskander period
steps.note: A long reign that stabilizes the late-Dhiyamigili frame before later succession turbulence.

steps.title: Short succession break
steps.note: A brief reign marks the start of rapid handovers in the same political generation.

steps.title: Competing branches
steps.note: Overlapping claims and branch competition become visible in kinship links.

steps.title: Restoration attempt
steps.note: Ghiyathuddine's accession is often treated as a corrective move in succession narratives.

steps.title: Year of rapid transfer
steps.note: Control passes quickly; uncertainty bands widen in some source traditions.

steps.title: Early Huraagey consolidation
steps.note: Muizzuddine anchors the line that later matures into the main late-monarchy dynasty.

steps.title: Institutional stabilization
steps.note: Nooredine's period helps normalize governance after repeated transitions.

steps.title: Long continuity reign
steps.note: Mueenuddine represents a long, comparatively stable sovereign phase.

steps.title: Nineteenth-century extension
steps.note: Imaduddine carries the line into a long 19th-century state-building period.

steps.title: Double accession period
steps.note: Ibrahim Nooredine's two accessions highlight recurring contest and restoration patterns.

steps.title: Late-monarchy high phase
steps.note: Shamsuddine III's long rule is central to final-monarchy constitutional change.

steps.title: Terminal monarchy transition
steps.note: The system enters its final succession phase before republican transformations.

steps.title: Final Sultan
steps.note: Farid's reign closes the monarchy before abolition in 1968.

storyTrails.title: Queens, Claimants, and Succession
storyTrails.summary: Follow major claim transitions around Khadijah and the early Hilaaly-era succession environment.

steps.title: Khadijah's accession cycle
steps.note: Multiple returns to rule make Khadijah a central node for 14th-century legitimacy debates.

steps.title: Intervening claimant
steps.note: Short intervening reign underscores active contest over sovereign office.

steps.title: Another short handover
steps.note: Brief succession windows indicate pressure between rival elite alignments.

steps.title: Raadhaafathi episode
steps.note: A transition point often treated as politically fragile in secondary chronologies.

steps.title: Maakurathu connection
steps.note: The succession merges with linked elite houses in the same period.

steps.title: Post-contest stabilization
steps.note: A longer reign follows repeated turnover, suggesting temporary institutional reset.

steps.title: Two-accession profile
steps.note: Ibrahim's two accessions preserve the pattern of restoration politics.

steps.title: Narrow transition interval
steps.note: Very short-duration sovereign nodes remain critical for accurate lineage mapping.

steps.title: Bridge to later dynastic formations
steps.note: Hadi Hassan sits in the transition corridor preceding clearer later-dynasty structuring.

storyTrails.title: Monarchy to Republic
storyTrails.summary: A compact constitutional narrative from the final sultanate phase to contemporary presidencies.

steps.title: Final monarchy phase
steps.note: Farid's reign defines the final decade of the sovereign crown.

steps.title: Majeed Didi context
steps.note: Prince-level modern reform context intersects with late-monarchy succession politics.

steps.title: First republic interval
steps.note: Amin Didi's presidency marks the first republican constitutional experiment.

steps.title: Second republic foundation
steps.note: Nasir leads the post-monarchy state order after abolition of the sultanate.

steps.title: Long republican consolidation
steps.note: Maumoon's long tenure defines a full-era institutional baseline.

steps.title: Transitional presidency
steps.note: Waheed's period marks a short but important transfer episode.

steps.title: Electoral reset
steps.note: Solih's period represents another democratic cycle realignment.

steps.title: Current presidency
steps.note: Muizzu anchors the present phase in the chronology.

storyTrails.title: Dhiyamigili South Branch
storyTrails.summary: Trace the documented southern Dhiyamigili-linked branch tied to Addu/Fuvahmulah research lines.

steps.title: Source-line sovereign anchor
steps.note: The branch trace starts from late Dhiyamigili sovereign context.

steps.title: Branch divergence context
steps.note: Collateral southern links are usually attached to post-contestation succession phases.

steps.title: Southern collateral bridge
steps.note: Documented as a bridge node in the Dhiyamigili-linked southern genealogy chain.

steps.title: Al-Nabeel Karayye Hassan Didi
steps.note: Another high-salience node in the same southern collateral line.

steps.title: Princess Aishath Didi
steps.note: Represents the downstream continuation of this southern branch in current research coverage.
